title: Why Journalists Are Going Indie (with Maddy Myers)

reason: Journalists building ownership structures outside platform capture is a concrete routing-level change in how knowledge and attention flow.
angle: Use routing architecture to examine how indie journalist collectives like Mothership restructure who controls distribution, revenue, and editorial autonomy — a flow-register shift away from platform intermediaries.

title: Websites break California privacy law at ‘industrial scale,’ survey finds
summary: Tech companies like Google, Facebook and Microsoft are ignoring data controls mandated under California law, researchers say.
reason: This story shows the capture mechanism in operation: companies encoding extraction into the infrastructure itself while using legal compliance as an alibi for legitimacy.
angle: Use 'alibi structure' to show how the existence of California privacy law functions as a legitimating alibi for ongoing industrial-scale extraction — the law's visibility as protection obscures its failure as protection.

title: After Decades of Quiet Rumbling, an Epidemic Is Erupting Among California Stoneworkers
summary: The engineered stone industry is worth nearly $30 billion. But the workers at its core are falling sick and dying from an illness called silicosis. Now these workers—most of whom are young immigrants—are suing manufacturers.
reason: This story shows enclosure in its rawest form: a multi-billion-dollar industry externalizing the biological costs of production onto undocumented immigrant workers whose precarity makes them structurally invisible to regulatory capture mechanisms.
angle: Use the alibi structure tool — the engineered stone industry's alibi is 'compliance with existing standards,' but the lawsuits are making visible that those standards were set while the industry knew about silicosis risk, collapsing the legitimacy of the regulatory framework itself.

title: Saving Culture at the Beeb
summary: The BBC is a key employer and a major creator of arts and entertainment, but neither role appears to matter yet in the ongoing review of its charter. That charter — the constitutional basis for the corporation, setting out its public purpose and governance — is now under review, with the government’s Green Paper published […]
reason: The BBC charter review exposes how a public commons institution gets re-routed toward market logic, making visible the alibi that public broadcasting serves public purpose while governance restructuring captures its productive substrate.
angle: Use the alibi structure tool: the charter's 'public purpose' language is the alibi for what is actually a routing decision about who controls the flows of cultural production and labor that the BBC depends on but cannot itself generate.

title: For Earth Day, the Trump Administration Wants To Expand Highways Across America

reason: This is a real policy shift with enclosure implications for shared urban space, but as framed it reads primarily as a representational critique (bad policy, wrong priorities) without surfacing the routing architecture or commons dynamics beneath it.

title: ‘Best Bikeshare in America’: An Unexpected Community Launches Free, All-Electric Micromobility For Residents
summary: Omaha and neighboring Council Bluffs, Iowa share an expansive e-bikeshare network that punches above its weight, supporters say — and now, it's free to all residents, too.
reason: A mid-sized metro eliminating fare barriers to an e-bikeshare network is a concrete routing-architecture story — redirecting mobility flows outside the car-ownership market and making the commons of urban movement materially accessible.
angle: Use the routing architecture tool to examine how removing the price barrier changes who can move through the city and what productive circuits become possible when mobility is treated as a commons rather than a commodity.

title: From Barcelona to Paris, cities thrive with women in charge. It’s all about sharing public space | Melissa and Chris Bruntlett
summary: Female mayors have had notable successes in making transport in urban areas benefit everyone, not just a wealthy few According to the UN 68% of the global population will be urban dwellers by mid-century. Urbanisation at this rate, unprecedented in modern times, means cities are facing an equally unprecedented convergence of crises, from a shortage of affordable housing to increased traffic congestion causing pollution, while reducing safety and liveability. The consequences are exacerbated by the climate emergency bombarding many regions with severe heatwaves, rainfall, flooding and other extreme weather events. While everyone will feel the effects of these changes, the fallout from failing to cater for them is disproportionately felt by the most vulnerable groups. Continue reading...
